unitTitle: KS2 Y5 Systems and searching (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Systems — parts that work together
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that a system is made of parts that work together
I can name the input, process and output of a digital system
I can explain that computers in a system talk to other devices
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Words on the board: system · part · input · process · output. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the activity worksheet ready. Read the three 'I can…' aloud, slowly.
1. (0–8) Starter — what is a system? Show the bicycle. A system is PARTS that work together to do a job. Pupils LABEL the bike parts (handlebars, wheel, pedals, seat) on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: match the part to its job.  • Core: name a part.  • Challenge: which parts work together to steer.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — a digital system. Show the washing machine. INPUT = you press a button / turn the dial. PROCESS = the machine does the wash. OUTPUT = clean clothes + the display. Likely error: 'the display is the input' → fix-words 'input is what YOU put in; output is what comes OUT.' Pupils SORT parts into input / process / output.
3. (22–40) You-do — design a talking teddy system. Show the teddy. Pupils choose the inside parts (button, speaker, battery, tiny computer) and ORDER the steps: squeeze paw → computer checks → speaker plays sound. They screenshot or draw their design onto the activity worksheet.
   • Support: choose the input and the output.  • Core: order the three steps.  • Challenge: add a second job (light up) and name its output.
4. (40–50) Computers talk to each other. Show the smart locker. Parts in different places send messages: you get a code → you key it in → the right door opens. Computers in the system talk to each other.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil names one input and one output on screen.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Computer systems and us
lessons.objectives:
I can name tasks that computer systems do for us
I can sort jobs into 'done by a person' and 'done by a computer'
I can name one good thing a computer system gives us
I can name ways to keep my information safe online
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: system · sensor · person · computer · online safety. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open. Read the four 'I can…' aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — systems around us. Pupils MULTI-SELECT the things that are run by a computer system (traffic lights, bus board, self-checkout, a paper book).
   • Support: tick the computer systems.  • Core: name one more system you have used.  • Challenge: say what the computer does in one of them.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — a road crossing system. A puffin crossing has a SENSOR (it can see people waiting). The sensor is the input. Stopping the cars is the output. Say one good thing the sensor gives us (cars do not wait for no one).
3. (22–40) You-do — the catalogue store (like Argos). Show the delivery van. A shop system has many jobs. Pupils SORT each job into 'a person does it' or 'a computer does it' on the activity worksheet, then ORDER how an order goes through the shop.
   • Support: sort 4 jobs.  • Core: order the steps.  • Challenge: which job changes if an item is sent back?
4. (40–50) Stay safe online. We use systems to stay safe online too: a password, a lock, not sharing private details. Pupils MULTI-SELECT what is safe to keep private.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil names one computer system they used today.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Searching the web
lessons.objectives:
I can use a search engine to find information
I can make a search better (refine it)
I can compare two search engines
I can spot a website I can trust
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: search · search engine · refine · trust. TA: internet devices ready; the starter worksheet open. Read the four 'I can…' aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — what is a search engine? It is a system: the input is the words you type, the output is the list of results. Pupils MULTI-SELECT the search engines they know (Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo, a calculator).
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — write good search steps. ORDER the steps of a search: open a browser → go to a search engine → type your words → press search → read the results. Then pupils write ONE clear search for a question on the worksheet.
3. (22–40) You-do — refine a search. Some words mean more than one thing. Show Rugby (a town AND a sport), Sale, and Boot. Pupils SORT what each search brings back, then make the search BETTER by adding a word (e.g. 'Rugby town'). Likely error: searching one word and stopping → fix-words 'add a word to say exactly what you mean.'
   • Support: choose the better search.  • Core: write a refined search.  • Challenge: explain why 'Boot Cumbria' is better than 'Boot'.
4. (40–50) Compare and trust. Search the same word in two search engines — are the results the same? A website you can TRUST: ends in things like .gov.uk or .ac.uk, looks tidy, no wild claims. Pupils MULTI-SELECT signs a site can be trusted.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil shows one search that worked.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Selecting search results
lessons.objectives:
I can explain why we need search engines to find things
I can explain what a web crawler does
I can explain how a search engine uses an index
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: index · crawler · bot · search engine. TA: a reference book with an index ready; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — so many websites. In 1991 there was 1 website. Today there are over a BILLION. Pupils answer: how do we find one page in a billion? They MULTI-SELECT good ways to find things fast.
2. (8–20) I-do — an index. Show a book index — it lists words and the page they are on, so you do not read every page. A search engine uses an index too. Pupils use a real book index to find a word.
3. (20–38) You-do — be a web crawler. A web crawler (a 'bot' / 'spider') is a PROGRAM that visits pages and writes down the words it finds in an index. Pupils make a small index of the classroom (item → where it is) on the activity worksheet, then ORDER how a search engine finds a page: crawler reads pages → writes words in the index → you type a word → it looks in the index → it shows the pages.
   • Support: match crawler / index / search to its job.  • Core: order the five steps.  • Challenge: why is looking in an index faster than reading every page?
4. (38–50) More words, more results. A word that is on many pages gives MORE results. Pupils explain in one line.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what a crawler does in their own words.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: How search results are ranked
lessons.objectives:
I can put a list in rank order
I can explain that a search engine follows rules to rank pages
I can name things that make a page rank higher
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: order · rank · rule · link. TA: A3 paper and pens; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — order and rank. Ranking means putting things in order, best first. Pupils ORDER four cities by size (biggest first) on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: choose which is biggest.  • Core: put four in order.  • Challenge: say why ranked order is more useful than A–Z here.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — how a search engine ranks. A search engine follows RULES (an algorithm) and gives each page a score. The page with the best score goes to the top. Show the search bar 'your school'.
3. (22–42) You-do — score a page. Pupils make a quick paper webpage about the school, then SCORE it using the rules: the search word in the heading = lots of points; in the URL = lots; another page links to you = lots. Add up the score on the activity worksheet and rank the pages.
   • Support: tick where the search word appears.  • Core: add up the score.  • Challenge: change your page to score higher and say what you changed.
4. (42–50) What helps a page rank? Pupils MULTI-SELECT the things that push a page UP the list.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil names one thing that raises a page's rank.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: How are searches influenced?
lessons.objectives:
I can name ways search results can be changed
I can name a limit of search engines
I can explain how search engines make money
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: influence · advert · sponsored · limit. TA: the starter worksheet open; the search-ads image on the board. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. (End-of-unit lesson — recap the journey at the end.)
1. (0–8) Starter — three people in a search. The SEARCHER (what you type), the SEARCH ENGINE (its rules and adverts), and the PAGE MAKER (the words they use) all change what you see. Pupils SORT three actions to the right person on the starter worksheet.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — adverts at the top. Show the 'guitar' results: the top items say Sponsored / Ad. These are PAID adverts. Pupils LABEL the advert parts on the activity worksheet (find the 'Sponsored' and the 'Ad' marks).
3. (22–38) You-do — how search engines make money. A search engine is free for you, so how does it make money? It SELLS the advert spots at the top. Pupils answer short questions and MULTI-SELECT true facts about adverts.
   • Support: choose how the search engine makes money.  • Core: explain why a shop pays to be at the top.  • Challenge: is the top result always the best one? Why not?
4. (38–48) Limits of search. Some things you cannot search well: a brand-new page not yet crawled, a private page, a fact no one has written down. Pupils MULTI-SELECT what a search engine cannot find.
5. (48–55) End of unit — recap the journey: system → internet as a system → search engine → crawler + index → ranking → adverts. Self-tick the three 'I can…'.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
